title: Check yourself: links, images & media
questions.0.q: They add an image. The file is bike.jpg and it lives in the uroci folder. They write src="bike.jpg". Result: a broken icon. Why?
questions.0.options.0: The name is wrong — it should be bike.jpeg
questions.0.options.1: A path is directions, not a name. "bike.jpg" means "next to me", but the image is in another folder
questions.0.options.2: The alt is missing, so the image will not load
questions.0.explain: The browser knows nothing about your folders. It knows addresses. Without a leading slash it looks for the file next to the page. With one, it starts from the site root: /uroci/bike.jpg.
questions.1.q: They write an address on their page: https://example.com. Does it look like a link — blue, underlined? No. It is black and does not click. What is missing?
questions.1.options.0: Nothing is missing — you just have to wait for it to load
questions.1.options.1: The address must be in <strong> to become active
questions.1.options.2: The address is just text. It becomes a link only inside <a href="...">
questions.1.explain: You recognise the address because you know what "https" means. The browser sees letters. Nothing is a link just because it looks like one.
questions.2.q: They added a video: <video src="/uroci/bike.mp4"></video>. The file exists, the path is right. A black rectangle appears with no buttons at all. What did they forget?
questions.2.options.0: controls — without it the browser gives no playback buttons
questions.2.options.1: autoplay — the video should start on its own
questions.2.explain: An image is looked at. A video is operated. The browser gives no buttons until you ask. The same goes for <audio> — without controls it is simply invisible.
questions.3.q: They make a menu: a paragraph with three links separated by | bars. It looks exactly like a menu and it works. What is wrong?
questions.3.options.0: Nothing — it works and it looks fine
questions.3.options.1: The bars are a drawing, not meaning. To a machine this is a paragraph with stray links; a menu is a list inside <nav>
questions.3.options.2: The links should be <strong> so they stand out
questions.3.explain: The same thing again: punctuation where a tag belongs. The right version looks uglier — a stacked, bulleted list. And it is still correct: laying it out flat is CSS work, and CSS has nothing to arrange if there is no list inside.
questions.4.q: A product photo. They write alt="". When is that RIGHT?
questions.4.options.1: When the image is pure decoration and carries no information at all
questions.4.options.2: When the image is already described in the paragraph next to it
questions.4.options.3: Never — alt must always have text
questions.4.explain: An empty alt is a deliberate message: "skip this, there is nothing here for you". For a flourish or a divider it is the right call. For a photo of the product you are selling it is not. The difference is whether you decided it or forgot it.
questions.5.q: They try to embed Google in their page with an <iframe>. They get an empty box. What happened?
questions.5.options.2: The site refused to be embedded. It has that right, and serious sites use it
questions.5.explain: The reason is theft: if I could embed your bank inside my page, I could fool you into thinking you were on their site while you typed your password. So you only embed what WANTS to be embedded — YouTube, maps, Spotify. They even hand you the code.
questions.6.q: They add a link to another site. The visitor clicks and is gone. How do they keep them?
questions.6.options.0: target="_blank" — opens the other page in a new tab
questions.6.options.1: They cannot. Once clicked, the visitor is lost
questions.6.options.2: By putting the link inside an <iframe>
questions.6.explain: By default a link opens the new page IN PLACE OF yours. Yours is gone. With target="_blank" it goes to a new tab and yours stays open.
questions.7.q: The image shows but it is enormous and blows up the page. They add width="400" and it looks fine. What is questionable about that?
questions.7.options.1: The number means nothing on another screen. Tomorrow the phone is 320 wide. The real answer is CSS
questions.7.options.2: 400 is too small for a photo
questions.7.options.3: width is not a valid attribute of
questions.7.explain:
The same trap as stacking
for air: it works, and that is exactly why it is a trap. A hard number solves today's screen and breaks tomorrow's. max-width: 100% in CSS says "be yourself, but never wider than the room you have" — and it holds everywhere.
